content.full_text:
Fern Polrrrcer- PRACTTcEs C oMMrssroN
P.O. Box 807 o 428 J Street o Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 o Fax (916) 322-0886
February 5,2002
Angie Zimmerman, Secretary-Treasurer
El Dorado Hills Incorporation Committee
3276 Stonehurst Drive
El Dorado Hills, CA95762
Re
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. A-01-204
Dear Ms. Zimmetman:
This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of the El Dorado
Hills Incorporation Committee regarding the reporting provisions of the Political Reform
Act (the "Act").I Please note that our assistan.L ir ti*ii"d to provisions of the Act and
does not address areas of the law outside the Act, such as Government Code Section
56300, which also contains lobbying disclosure and reporting requirements.
QUESTION
When must your grassroots committee comprised of members of a community
seeking the right to vote for city incorporation through the LAFCO process file a
Statement of Organization and begin reporting contributions under the Act?
CONCLUSION
The committee seeking the right to vote for city incorporation through the
LAFCO process must file its Statement of Organization (Form 410) within 10 days of
receiving contributions totaling $1,000 or more in a calendar year,but the 1O-day filing
period does not begin to run until the necessary legislative body approves the placement
of the incorporation issue on the ballot.
I Govenrment Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appeff at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.


File No. A-01-204
Page No. 2
FACTS
The El Dorado Hills Incorporation Committee ("EDHINC") is comprised of a
group of citizens who live in El Dorado Hills. EDHINC is a grassroots effort to gain the
right to vote on city incorporation for El Dorado Hills. EDHINC is also involved in the
LAFCO process for incorporation, as set forth in the Government Code. This process
beganin 1997. Under the LAFCO requirementsi as set forth in the Government Code,
the proponents of the incorporation must collect at least 25o/o of the signatures from
residents to initiate the LAFCO incorporation process. This was done in L997.
After submission and the initial petition, LAFCO follows the following statutory
process: LAFCO prepares a comprehensive fiscal analysis to determine if the proposed
new city would be fiscally feasible and it prepares an environmental analysis of the
proposed new city under the California Environmental Quality Act ("CEeA'). Upon
completion of these analyses, LAFCO holds public hearings and then determines, in its
discretion, whether the incorporation should be placed before the voters within the
proposed city. Only if LAFCO approves the incorporation for submission to the voters is
the incorporation measure then placed on the ballot.
LAFCO requires the proponents of the incorporation to pay for all of LAFCO's
processing costs, including the fiscal analysis and the CEQA review. These reports and
other processing requirements arc very costly. Many of the committee members have put
up their own money, entertainment books have been sold to raise money, and you need
an additional $235,000 to complete the LAFCO process. Again, all of these funds will be
expended prior to a decision on whether the incorporation will be placed before the voters
and prior to the call of an election.
Pursuant to our telephone conversation of Decemb er !7 , 20Ol , EDHINC is no
longer operating, and the LAFCO process is no longer moving forward.
ANALYSIS
The Act provides that campaign disclosure obligations will be incurred by persons
who make payments for political purposes. (Sections 82013 and 82025.) A payment is
made for political purposes if it is intended to influence the actions of the voters for or
against the qualification or passage of any measure. (Regulation r\2z5.)
The Commission has held that aproponent's obligation to report expenditures in
support of a proposal begins when the proposal becomes a "measure ." (In re Fontana
(1976) 2 FPPC Ops. 25.) With regard to incorporation matters, that does not occur until
the appropriate legislative body, typically the county board of supervisors, votes to place
the proposal on the ballot. (Id. at 28.) However, the Commission also clearly stated that
although the proponent need not file reports until that time, previous contributions and
expenditures may need to be recaptured on the first campaign report.
The initial campaign statement must include contributions received and
expenditures made in anticipation of the measure being placed on the ballot, even if such


File No. A-01-204
Page No. 3
contributions and expenditures were made before the board of supervisors actually placed
the proposal on the ballot. (Id. at 28 n. 5.)
Monies received and spent by a proponent in gathering petitions to submit to
LAFCO are not reportable under the Act. However, contributions and expenditures that
predate the filing of the first campaign statement are reportable if they were intended to
influence voters to vote for incorporation. (Martini Advice Letter, No. A-93-378.)
A proponent seeking the right to vote for city incorporation through the LAFCO
process must file its Statement of Organization(Form 410) within 10 days of receiving
contributions totaling $1,000 or more in a calendar year. However, the 10-day filing
period does not begin to run until the necessary legislative body approves the placement
of the incorporation issue on the ballot. Since your grassroots committee is no longer
operating, you will have no obligation to file unless and until EDHINC is revived and the
LAFCO process resumes.
Enclosed for your information is a copy of In re Fontana, (1976) 2 FPPC Op.25,
and a copy of a memorandum dated August 3l,2OOl, from then Acting Executive
Director Mark Krausse to the Commission regarding the status of the Los Angeles City
Ethics Commission's request to revisit In re Fontana. This memorandum contains the
Commission's most recent statement regarding the reporting of contributions and
expenditures in the LAFCO measure context.
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (e16)
322-5660
HBAjg
I:\Adviceltrs\A-0 1 -204
Enclosures
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel
By: Holly B. Armstrong
Staff Counsel, Legal Division

When must your grassroots committee comprised of members of a community
seeking the right to vote for city incorporation through the LAFCO process file a
Statement of Organization and begin reporting contributions under the Act?

The committee seeking the right to vote for city incorporation through the

receiving contributions totaling $1,000 or more in a calendar year,but the 1O-day filing
period does not begin to run until the necessary legislative body approves the placement
of the incorporation issue on the ballot.

QUESTION: When must your grassroots committee comprised of members of a community
seeking the right to vote for city incorporation through the LAFCO process file a

CONCLUSION: The committee seeking the right to vote for city incorporation through the
LAFCO process must file its Statement of Organization (Form 410) within 10 days of
receiving contributions totaling $1,000 or more in a calendar year,but the 1O-day filing
period does not begin to run until the necessary legislative body approves the placement
of the incorporation issue on the ballot.
